claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 21 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 11 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 21, which is 20 or over
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 11 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 21, which is 20 or over; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm here to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let me start by gathering some information about your situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To begin, could you please tell me:

**Do you have a child (or children) that you're responsible for?** If yes, could you tell me their age(s)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have children I'm responsible for. I have three children actually - Alex who is 21, Blake who is 15, and Charlie who is 11.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. You have three children:
- Alex (21 years old)
- Blake (15 years old)  
- Charlie (11 years old)

I'll need to assess eligibility for each child separately, as Child Benefit is determined on a per-child basis.

Let me start with **Alex, who is 21 years old**. Unfortunately, children aged 20 or over do not qualify for Child Benefit, so Alex would not be eligible.

Now let's look at **Blake (15 years old)**. Since Blake is under 16, I need to ask:

**Is Blake working in paid employment for 24 hours or more per week?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work 24 hours or more per week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Since Blake is under 16 and not working 24 hours or more per week, Blake potentially qualifies.

Now let's check about **Charlie (11 years old)**. Since Charlie is also under 16, I need to ask:

**Is Charlie working in paid employment for 24 hours or more per week?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie doesn't work either - they're only 11!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Of course! That makes sense. So both Blake and Charlie are not working 24 hours or more per week.

Now I need to ask about your relationship to the children and your living situation:

**Do Blake and Charlie normally live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Charlie lives with me, but Blake doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So Charlie lives with you, but Blake does not.

Let me ask about Blake first, since Blake doesn't live with you:

**Do you contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit would pay towards Blake's upkeep?** (This would be at least the equivalent of the weekly Child Benefit rate, which is £25.60 for the eldest eligible child or £16.95 for additional children.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute about £10 a week towards Blake's upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Unfortunately, since you contribute £10 per week towards Blake's upkeep, which is less than the Child Benefit rate (£16.95 per week for an additional child), you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Now let me ask a few more questions about **Charlie, who lives with you**:

**Are you and Charlie both normally living in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, we both live in the UK - we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just a few more questions about Charlie:

**Is Charlie currently in local authority care, or have they been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie isn't in care at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Now, one final question about your own circumstances:

**Are you responsible for Charlie? This means you're the person who is primarily responsible for Charlie's day-to-day care and decisions.**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. Charlie lives with me and I'm responsible for their day-to-day care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me summarize what we've discussed and give you my assessment for each child:

**Alex (21 years old):** Not eligible for Child Benefit because Alex is over 20 years old. Child Benefit only covers children under 16, or under 20 if they're in approved education or training.

**Blake (15 years old):** Not eligible for Child Benefit in your case. Although Blake is under 16 and not working full-time (which would normally qualify), Blake doesn't live with you. When a child doesn't live with you, you can only claim Child Benefit if you contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards their upkeep. Since you contribute £10 per week, which is less than the Child Benefit rate (£16.95 per week), you don't meet this requirement.

**Charlie (11 years old):** **You ARE eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.** Charlie meets all the qualifying criteria:
- Under 16 years old
- Not working 24 hours or more per week
- Lives with you normally
- You're responsible for Charlie's day-to-day care
- Both of you live in the UK
- Charlie is not in local authority care

**Important note about the High Income Child Benefit Charge:** If you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through the tax system, but you're still eligible to claim it.

Would you like information about how to apply for Child Benefit for Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's really helpful, thank you! Yes, I'd appreciate information on how to apply for Charlie. I'm glad at least one of them qualifies!
